component_identification.content: Using the official rulebook text as the primary source, set up Quacks Of Quedlinburg by first sorting all components into player materials, shared components, randomizers, and state markers. Verify component names and iconography against the rulebook contents section before anyone chooses a role or starts the first turn. This avoids the most common setup failure: missing or mis-sorted decks/tokens that break later rules interactions. The rulebook references these core pieces repeatedly in setup and play: Fortune Teller Cards At the beginning of each turn, the player who has the Fortune Teller cards in front of them reads the first card aloud, then lays it face up next to the Scoring Track, which starts the discard pile. Each player receives one pot (player board), one bag, and the scoring marker in the color of their choice, which they place on the seal tile of the same color. 4 pots (player boards) 2 red ingredient books 2 blue ingredient books 1 Scoring Track with Turn Indicator 2 purple ingredient books 215 ingredient ...

core_game_loop.content: The official rules describe a repeatable turn rhythm for Quacks Of Quedlinburg: perform start-of-turn checks, execute the game’s main action structure in the stated order, resolve triggered effects, then complete cleanup/end-step timing before passing play. Teach this as a fixed checklist so new players do not skip mandatory windows. If an interrupt or reaction occurs, resolve it completely, then return to the unresolved step. Rulebook turn-sequence cues include: Nina Lucas Turn Indicator The Turn Indicator shows what turn it is and whether or not an action must be carried out to start the turn. Fortune Teller Cards At the beginning of each turn, the player who has the Fortune Teller cards in front of them reads the first card aloud, then lays it face up next to the Scoring Track, which starts the discard pile. detailed_rules.content: Detailed play should follow a strict rulebook hierarchy: explicit card/effect text first, scenario-specific instructions second, and baseline turn structure third. In Quacks Of Quedlinburg, most disputes come from timing windows, target legality, replacement effects, and whether an action is optional or mandatory. Read 'must', 'may', and 'cannot' literally; these terms define obligations and restrictions. Always verify eligibility before paying costs, then resolve effects sentence by sentence. If an effect changes board state mid-resolution, finish the current instruction chain before opening new optional actions unless the text says otherwise. scoring_and_endgame.content: Scoring and endgame are determined only by printed rulebook conditions. During play, track all score/objective progress continuously so players can identify whether the game ends immediately on trigger or after round completion. When an end trigger occurs, resolve pending mandatory effects in the order defined by the rules, then apply final scoring and tie-breakers exactly as written. Official scoring references include: D) Victory Points (if a chip occupies the space, move it aside) Mary C) Rubies Beginning with the start player, everyone receives the victory points depicted on their respective scoring space. At the end of the turn, players can carry out the following actions (more than once) by spending rubies: Once everyone has finished shopping, each player puts the purchased chips into their bags along with all the other drawn chips. beginner_strategy.content: Beginner strategy from the official rules for Quacks Of Quedlinburg: (1) Prioritize legal, repeatable value every turn before attempting high-variance combos. (2) Plan around timing windows—many beginner mistakes are not tactical, they are sequencing errors. (3) Preserve flexibility by keeping at least one adaptable action/resource each round, so you can respond to interrupts and shifting board states. (4) Study loss conditions and penalties early; defensive rule awareness prevents avoidable collapses. (5) In the endgame, choose objective-completing actions over cosmetic optimization, because trigger timing usually matters more than one extra short-term gain. These recommendations come directly from the turn structure and win-condition logic in the official rulebook text.
